entries.title: I got a Centrelink decision or debt I disagree with

reviewable.basis: You can ask Services Australia to look at most Centrelink decisions again (an internal review by an Authorised Review Officer). If you still disagree, you can usually apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal (ART).

pathways.name: Ask Services Australia to explain or look at the decision again (internal review)
pathways.body: Services Australia — an Authorised Review Officer (ARO)
pathways.deadline: You can usually ask for an internal review, but asking sooner can matter for any back-payment. A time limit may apply to some steps — confirm the current rule and how it is counted for your decision.
pathways.howToStart: tell Services Australia you disagree and ask for the decision to be reviewed by an Authorised Review Officer

pathways.cost: applying for review of a Centrelink decision is usually free
pathways.name: Apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal (ART) for external review
pathways.deadline: There is a time limit to apply to the ART for many decisions. Confirm the current period and how it is counted before you apply, because missing it can limit your options.
pathways.howToStart: apply to the ART for review, usually after the internal review

rightToReasons.how: you can ask Services Australia for a written statement of the reasons for the decision

groundsOrCriteria: you think the decision got the facts wrong (for example, the income or dates used)
you think a debt was worked out incorrectly
you think the rules were applied incorrectly to your situation
your circumstances were not properly taken into account
evidenceChecklist: the decision letter or the debt notice, including any reference number
payslips, bank statements or records showing your income and the dates
letters from a doctor, caseworker or support service, if relevant
any letters or messages you have had from Services Australia about the decision

getHelp.who: free, independent help with Centrelink decisions and debts
ask for a decision to be reviewed
independent external review of Centrelink decisions
free, independent financial counselling — 1800 007 007

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: If you got a Centrelink decision or debt and you disagree with it, you can usually ask Services Australia to look at it again — this is called an internal review, done by an Authorised Review Officer. If you still disagree after that, you can usually apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal (ART), which is independent of Centrelink. Time limits can apply to some steps, so it helps to act early and get free help. Free welfare-rights services can explain your options.
sources: Services Australia — Reviews and appeals — servicesaustralia.gov.au
entries.body:
Most Centrelink decisions can be looked at again. The first step is usually an internal
review by Services Australia, done by an Authorised Review Officer. If you still disagree,
you can usually apply to the Administrative Review Tribunal, which is independent of
Centrelink. Time limits can apply to some steps, so it helps to act early and get free help.

This is a draft entry. The steps, time limits and laws here must be checked by a lawyer
before launch. No exact time limit is shown until it is confirmed.

entries.title: I got a fine or infringement notice in Victoria

reviewable.basis: You can ask for a review on set grounds, nominate the real driver, ask to go to court, or get help to pay. What you can do depends on the stage your fine has reached.

pathways.howCounted: before the fine is registered (before a Notice of Final Demand)
pathways.name: Ask for a review (on one of the five grounds)
pathways.body: the agency that issued the fine, or Fines Victoria
pathways.deadline: You can usually ask before your fine is registered — that is, before a 'Notice of Final Demand'. You can only ask for one review per fine.
pathways.howToStart: apply for a review and choose a ground
pathways.source: fines.vic.gov.au — request a review; Infringements Act 2006 (Vic)

pathways.howCounted: from the day you found out about the fine
pathways.name: Ask for a review because you did not know about the fine
pathways.body: the agency that issued the fine, or Fines Victoria
pathways.deadline: Apply within 14 days of finding out about the fine.
pathways.howToStart: apply for a review on the 'person was unaware' ground (you may need a statutory declaration)
pathways.source: fines.vic.gov.au — if you didn't know about your fine

pathways.cost: no fee to ask. Going to court can lead to a different penalty, so free legal help is available.
pathways.howCounted: for serious driving offences, from the date your infringement notice was issued
pathways.name: Ask to have the matter heard in court
pathways.body: Magistrates' Court of Victoria (Children's Court if you are under 18)
pathways.deadline: For excessive-speed, drink-driving or drug-driving offences: within 28 days of the notice. For other fines: before the fine is registered (before a Notice of Final Demand).
pathways.howToStart: ask to have the matter heard in court
pathways.source: fines.vic.gov.au — go to court; Infringements Act 2006 (Vic)

pathways.name: Get help to pay or clear the fine
pathways.howToStart: ask for a payment plan, or ask about a Work and Development Permit if you are in hardship (for example illness, disability, addiction, homelessness or family violence)
pathways.source: fines.vic.gov.au — pay by instalments; Work and Development Permit

rightToReasons.how: after a review, the agency must tell you the outcome in writing

groundsOrCriteria: you did not know about the fine (apply within 14 days of finding out)
the decision to fine you was contrary to law
it was a mistake of identity — you were not the person responsible
exceptional circumstances explain what happened, such as a medical emergency
special circumstances: at the time you had a mental illness or disability, a serious addiction, were homeless, or were affected by family violence, and this affected your behaviour
evidenceChecklist: the fine or notice, including the infringement number
for 'did not know': a statutory declaration about when and how you found out
for mistake of identity: proof you were not the driver or owner
for special circumstances: letters or reports from a doctor, caseworker or support service
any letters showing what stage your fine has reached

getHelp.who: manage, review or get help with a Victorian fine — (03) 9200 8111
free legal help with fines — Legal Help line 1300 792 387
free legal help for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people — 1800 064 865
free, independent financial counselling — 1800 007 007

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: If you got a fine or infringement notice in Victoria and you disagree with it, or you can't pay, you have options. You can ask for a review on set grounds, nominate the real driver, ask to go to court, or get help to pay. What you can do depends on the stage your fine has reached, so it helps to act early, before it becomes a 'Notice of Final Demand'. Free help is available.
sources: Victoria Legal Aid — Fines and infringements — legalaid.vic.gov.au
entries.body:
Figures here were checked against the cited Victorian sources on 2026-06-16. A
Victorian legal professional should confirm them before public launch.

Victorian fines move through stages: an Infringement Notice, then a Penalty Reminder
Notice, then a Notice of Final Demand, then enforcement. Some options — like going to
court or nominating another driver — are only available before the fine is registered
(before the Notice of Final Demand). The exact date to pay is the due date printed on
your own notice, so check the notice itself.

entries.title: A Victorian government decision I disagree with

reviewable.basis: Many Victorian government decisions can be reviewed at VCAT, but not all — it depends on whether the Act the decision was made under gives VCAT that power. Your decision letter should say if you can apply to VCAT.

pathways.howCounted: from the day the decision was made
pathways.name: Ask for the reasons in writing (a statement of reasons)
pathways.body: the decision-maker (the agency that made the decision)
pathways.deadline: Ask within 28 days of the decision. The decision-maker then usually has 28 days to give you written reasons.
pathways.howToStart: write to the decision-maker asking for a written statement of reasons under section 45 of the VCAT Act
pathways.source: Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal Act 1998 (Vic) ss 45-46; vcat.vic.gov.au

pathways.cost: a fee usually applies, but fee waivers and concessions are available
pathways.howCounted: usually from the decision, or from when you get the statement of reasons
pathways.name: Apply to VCAT to review the decision (if the law allows it)
pathways.deadline: Often 28 days, counted from the decision or from when you get the reasons — but the law that applies can set a different, sometimes shorter, limit. Check the time limit on your decision letter.
pathways.howToStart: check your decision letter for a review right and the Act it names, then apply to VCAT to review the decision
pathways.source: Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal Act 1998 (Vic) s 70; vcat.vic.gov.au — application for review of a decision

pathways.name: Complain to the Victorian Ombudsman
pathways.deadline: No fixed deadline, though the Ombudsman may not look at complaints older than 12 months. Complain to the organisation first.
pathways.howToStart: complain to the organisation first, then to the Victorian Ombudsman if you are still unhappy

rightToReasons.how: you can ask the decision-maker, in writing, for a statement of reasons within 28 days of the decision
rightToReasons.provision: Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal Act 1998 (Vic) s 45

groundsOrCriteria: if VCAT can review your decision, it usually takes a fresh look and can make its own decision in place of the original one — not just check whether the rules were followed
review is about what the correct or preferable decision is on the facts and the law
evidenceChecklist: the decision letter or notice, and the date you received it
any statement of reasons, or your dated request for one
the name of the Act and any section mentioned in your letter
forms and letters you sent the agency, and their replies
documents that support your side, such as records, reports or photos

getHelp.who: free legal information and advice — Legal Help line 1300 792 387
free local legal help — find your nearest centre
free complaints about Victorian government bodies (cannot overturn a decision)
self-help guides on applying to review a decision

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: If a Victorian government agency or council made a decision you disagree with, your options depend on the law behind the decision. Many decisions can be reviewed by VCAT, but not all. Your decision letter should say if you can apply, and by when. Asking for the reasons in writing within 28 days is one option, and it can help protect your time to apply. If you think an agency acted unfairly, you can also complain to the Victorian Ombudsman. Free legal help can explain what applies to you.
sources: Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal Act 1998 (Vic) — legislation.vic.gov.au
VCAT — Application for review of a decision — vcat.vic.gov.au
entries.body:
Figures here were checked against the cited Victorian sources on 2026-06-16. A
Victorian legal professional should confirm them before public launch.

This is the general guide for a Victorian state-government decision when there isn't a
more specific one. The most important point is honest uncertainty: VCAT can only review
a decision if the Act it was made under gives VCAT that power, so the decision letter
itself is the best guide to whether review is available and by when. The 28-day VCAT
review limit is a common default but can be shorter under a particular Act — so the safe
step is to check the letter and get free legal help quickly. The Victorian Ombudsman can
look into unfair treatment but cannot overturn or change a decision.

entries.title: I disagree with a public or community housing decision in Victoria
decisionTypes: removal from the Victorian Housing Register
issuers: Department of Families, Fairness and Housing

reviewable.basis: Most social-housing decisions can be challenged, but the route depends on the type of decision: housing-application decisions go to the Housing Appeals Office, while an eviction goes to VCAT.

pathways.howCounted: as soon as possible after the decision
pathways.name: Ask for a housing appeal (for decisions about your application, priority, transfer or rebate)
pathways.body: the Housing Appeals Office (an independent reviewer within the housing department)
pathways.deadline: There is no strict published deadline, but lodge as soon as you can. If the first review does not fix it, it automatically goes to the Housing Appeals Office for an independent review.
pathways.howToStart: talk to your local housing office first, then lodge a housing appeal online, by post or by phone
pathways.source: housing.vic.gov.au — appeal a decision; Housing Act 1983 (Vic)

pathways.cost: an application fee may apply, but fee waivers and concessions are available
pathways.howCounted: from the day you receive the notice to vacate
pathways.name: Challenge a notice to vacate (eviction) at VCAT
pathways.deadline: Apply within 30 days to challenge the notice early. If you miss that, you can still argue the notice is not valid at the possession hearing.
pathways.howToStart: apply to VCAT to challenge the notice
pathways.source: Residential Tenancies Act 1997 (Vic) s 91ZZS; vcat.vic.gov.au; tenantsvic.org.au

pathways.name: Community housing: complain to the provider, then the Housing Registrar
pathways.body: your community housing provider, then the Victorian Housing Registrar
pathways.deadline: Use the provider's complaints process first. If you are not happy with the outcome, you can ask the Housing Registrar to look at it.
pathways.howToStart: use the provider's internal complaints process, then contact the Housing Registrar

rightToReasons.how: if your application is not approved you get a letter explaining why, and a notice to vacate must state the reason

groundsOrCriteria: for housing decisions: whether the housing department applied its policies and procedures correctly
for an eviction: whether the notice to vacate is valid
for an eviction: whether it is reasonable and proportionate to make you leave — VCAT must weigh the impact on you and your household
family violence or serious hardship affecting your housing
evidenceChecklist: the decision letter or notice, and the date you received it
documents showing you meet the housing rules, such as income, residency, or medical, disability or family-violence evidence
for an eviction: evidence of hardship — children or dependants, health, disability, family violence, how long you have lived there, and any plan to pay arrears
any letters between you and the housing office or provider

getHelp.who: free advice for public, community and rooming-house renters — 1800 068 860
free legal help for people at risk of homelessness, including evictions — 1800 606 313
free legal help and duty lawyers — Legal Help line 1300 792 387
free, independent reviews of housing decisions — 1800 807 702

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: If you live in or applied for social housing in Victoria and you disagree with a decision, what you can do depends on the type of decision. Decisions about your application, priority, a transfer or your rebate can be appealed for free through the Housing Appeals Office. An eviction notice is different — it goes to VCAT, and there is a 30-day time limit to challenge it early. Free help is available.
sources: Victorian housing department — Appeal a decision (Housing Appeals Office) — housing.vic.gov.au
entries.body:
Figures here were checked against the cited Victorian sources on 2026-06-16. A
Victorian legal professional should confirm them before public launch.

Social-housing problems split into two separate paths, and getting into the right one
matters. Decisions about a housing *application* — eligibility, priority, a transfer, or
a rebate — are reviewed by the **Housing Appeals Office** and have no strict deadline.
An *eviction* (a notice to vacate) is a tenancy matter under the Residential Tenancies
Act, goes to **VCAT**, and has a 30-day window to challenge it early. The 30-day window
is the time to challenge *early* — a renter can still argue the notice is invalid at the
possession hearing.

entries.title: I'm renting in Victoria and got a notice or a rent increase I disagree with

reviewable.basis: Renters can challenge a notice to vacate and an excessive rent increase, and can resolve bond, repairs and compensation disputes for free.

pathways.cost: an application fee may apply, but fee waivers and concessions are available
pathways.howCounted: from the day you receive the notice to vacate
pathways.name: Challenge a notice to vacate at VCAT
pathways.deadline: Apply within 30 days to challenge the notice early. If you miss that, you can still argue the notice is not valid at the possession hearing, and you can ask for more time.
pathways.howToStart: apply to VCAT to challenge the notice (the case type is 'challenge a notice to vacate')
pathways.source: Residential Tenancies Act 1997 (Vic) s 91ZZS; consumer.vic.gov.au — challenging a notice to vacate; vcat.vic.gov.au

pathways.howCounted: from the day the rental provider gave you the written rent-increase notice
pathways.name: Ask Consumer Affairs Victoria to check an excessive rent increase
pathways.deadline: Ask within 30 days for a free rent assessment.
pathways.howToStart: ask Consumer Affairs Victoria for a free rent assessment
pathways.source: consumer.vic.gov.au — challenging rent increases or high rent

pathways.howCounted: from the day you get the rent assessment report
pathways.name: Take a rent, bond, repairs or compensation dispute to Rental Dispute Resolution Victoria
pathways.body: Rental Dispute Resolution Victoria (RDRV), which can refer it to VCAT
pathways.deadline: If you got a rent assessment report, apply within 30 days of getting the report. Bond, repairs and compensation disputes can also be brought here.
pathways.source: consumer.vic.gov.au — challenging rent increases or high rent; rdrv.vic.gov.au

rightToReasons.how: a notice to vacate must be on the correct form and give a valid reason — if it isn't, it may not be valid, and a rent increase must use the correct form

groundsOrCriteria: the notice is on the wrong or out-of-date form, or is missing required information
the notice period is too short for the reason given
the reason on the notice is not a lawful reason, or is not genuine (for example, a 'sale' notice when there is no real plan to sell)
the notice was given because you asked for repairs or stood up for your rights
for a rent increase: it came less than 12 months after the last one, the notice was too short, or the rent is high compared with similar nearby homes and the condition of the place
evidenceChecklist: the notice or letter itself, so you can check the date, the form and the reason
your rental agreement (lease) and any condition report
your rent payment records
messages with the rental provider or agent, including when you got the notice
photos of the condition of the place and any repairs needed
the envelope or postmark showing when the notice arrived

getHelp.who: free advice for Victorian renters — Rental Support Line (03) 9416 2577
free help to resolve bond, rent, repairs and compensation disputes — 1300 017 378
free legal help and duty lawyers — Legal Help line 1300 792 387
free renting information and rent assessments

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: If you rent in Victoria and you got a notice to vacate or a rent increase you think is unfair, you have options. You can ask VCAT to check whether a notice to vacate is valid. You can ask Consumer Affairs Victoria for a free check of a rent increase. Bond, repairs and rent disputes can be sorted out for free through Rental Dispute Resolution Victoria. There are time limits, so it helps to act early, and free help is available.
sources: Consumer Affairs Victoria — Challenging a notice to vacate; Challenging rent increases or high rent — consumer.vic.gov.au
entries.body:
Figures here were checked against the cited Victorian sources on 2026-06-16. A
Victorian legal professional should confirm them before public launch.

Two recent changes matter when reading a renter's letter: since June 2025, bond,
rent-increase, repairs and compensation disputes start at **Rental Dispute Resolution
Victoria** (which can refer them to VCAT), while evictions and notices to vacate still
go to **VCAT**. From 25 November 2025 the minimum notice period for a rent increase
changed from 60 to 90 days, so an older letter may lawfully use the shorter period.

The 30-day window to challenge a notice to vacate is the time to challenge it *early* —
it is not the only chance. A renter can still argue a notice is invalid at the
possession hearing, so missing 30 days does not end their options.
